channel.title: Philosophy on Entropic Legos
channel.description: Recent content in Philosophy on Entropic Legos

item.title: The Vicious Regress of Vicious Regresses
item.author: hugo@example.com (Entropic Legos)
item.description:
Philosophy, in my experience, is littered with imprecise articulation and the resulting confabulation by even its most prominent practitioners. This bugs me, and I’ve started doing what I can to remedy this condition.

The Infinite Regress Argument

Take, for example, the infinite regress argument. This is the favourite toolkit in the philosopher’s arsenal for a proof by contradiction. The general form taken by the argument is this: consider the claim that there are certain conditions a proposition P must fulfil to be valid, call them Q. Then the proposition “Q validates P” also requires a separate validator, and this goes on ad infinitum.

item.title: Understanding Plato's Republic
item.author: hugo@example.com (Entropic Legos)
item.description:
Understanding Plato’s Republic - E1

Introduction

This the first episode in (hopefully) a series of several articles where I narrate my own understanding of The Republic, the magnum opus of Plato. In this episode, I discuss about the very initial parts of chapter 1 of the book. I give an overview of what transpires before Thrasymachus (while Socrates’ dialogues contain a lot of different characters much like a play, the exact names of them are not so important to take note of except for a few prominent figures that play a crucial role in Socrates’ arguments), the most vocal and prominent interlocutor of Socrates in chapter 1, starts engaging with Socrates. (This covers up to page 14 of the Penguin Classics translation.)
